narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your undergraduate studies and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your law school application didn't make the cut based on our model's assessment of your bar exam prospects. Picture this model as a wise counselor who's seen patterns in thousands of students' journeys, and it looked at your story and saw a 59.9% chance of stumbling on the bar exam, enough to pause before inviting you in. The biggest red flag for it was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you at the very bottom of your class that year, which screamed "struggle under pressure" louder than anything else. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 26.0 stood out as a hurdle, suggesting the kind of analytical sharpness the bar demands might not come as naturally. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to that narrative, showing early challenges that didn't fully turn around. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.6, your middle family income bracket hinted at fewer resources for the intense support many successful bar-takers lean on. As an Asian female going full-time, those traits together painted a picture of someone facing steeper odds in this high-stakes path, pulling the prediction toward failure in ways the model couldn't ignore. It's not that your target law school dream is out of reach—far from it—but this combo just tipped the scales. The good news? Year 3 slumps can be rebounds waiting to happen; maybe retaking the LSAT or bolstering those later grades could rewrite your story for the next application. You've got resilience in your profile already—hold onto that. We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong.
